prompt:
Set up a Community Giving & Fund Transparency Portal that enables local nonprofits to run project-based donation drives, disclose how money is used, and close the loop with supporter engagement and accountable fulfillment proof. The system serves four roles: Supporter (browses projects, donates, follows updates), Project Manager (creates projects, posts spending updates, responds to tickets), Finance Reviewer (verifies receipts and approves disclosures), and Administrator (governs risk controls and audits). In the Leptos web interface, supporters can search and filter projects by cause, status, and ZIP code, view a budget-versus-actual progress bar, and make either a project donation or a designated donation earmarked for a named budget line; the UI immediately confirms a pledge number and provides an on-screen receipt that can be printed. Supporters can favorite projects, like updates, leave comments, and submit feedback forms; a personal message center shows notifications with read/unread states, subscription controls per project, and Do Not Disturb hours (default 9:00 PM–7:00 AM). Staff users work in an operations back office with dashboards for GMV, conversion rate, average donation amount, and repeat donor rate over selectable date ranges, including multidimensional filtering and CSV export; sensitive actions such as refund approval, project unpublish, and comment removal require a second confirmation and re-entering the password.

The backend uses Axum to expose offline-first REST APIs to the Leptos frontend, enforcing signed authentication, rate limiting (for example, 60 requests per minute per session), and replay protection via short-lived nonces (5-minute validity). SQLite persists users, roles, projects, donation transactions, budget lines, expense records, receipts, comments, favorites, tickets, notifications, and an immutable operations log; personally identifiable information is minimized and masked in exports, while passwords are salted and hashed and sensitive notes are encrypted at rest using an application-managed key stored on the host machine. Because the deployment is fully offline, “payments” are recorded as local cash/check/card-terminal entries with optional reversal records rather than online processing; refunds are handled as negative adjustments that require Finance Reviewer approval and are reflected in public ledgers immediately after approval. Receipt/voucher uploads are validated locally by file type/size (PDF/JPG/PNG, max 10 MB) and fingerprinted to prevent duplicates; Finance Review marks them Verified/Rejected with mandatory rejection reasons. Engagement is protected by configurable sensitive-word filtering and risk-control toggles that can disable new comments or require pre-moderation. Fulfillment verification records arrival/start/end checkpoints with OTP or QR codes generated in-app and expiring after 10 minutes, plus time-consistency rules (start must be within 2 hours of arrival; end after start) to deter fraud; completion generates a tamper-evident service record and downloadable proof. Event instrumentation uses a unified local event model for impressions, clicks, dwell time, and session attribution, deduplicates repeated events within 3 seconds, flags suspicious bursts for admin review, and tracks data quality metrics in the KPI console. Webhooks are supported only for on-prem devices (such as door controllers or local terminals) on the same network, signed and retried up to 3 times with exponential backoff, and every outbound attempt is logged for audit.
